data.title: Second World War veteran’s local news empire sold after 66 years
data.description:
A local news empire built by a Second World War veteran has been sold 66 years after its launch.

Tindle Newspapers, which publishes 30 titles across southern England and Wales, has been sold by its family owners, marking the end of an era for one of the UK’s oldest local news groups.

The business was established by Sir Ray Tindle, the late newspaper entrepreneur, whose first foray into publishing came when he launched a newspaper aboard his troop ship while sailing to serve in the Far East during the Second World War.

In 1960, he used his demobilisation savings to buy the Balham and Tooting Gazette before building an empire that includes local titles such as the Cornish & Devon Post, the Tenby Observer, the Farnham Herald and the Isle of Man Courier.

Sir Ray, who died at 95 in 2022, was a passionate advocate for local journalism, arguing that his titles should focus solely on stories and events that mattered to the community.

In an interview in 2018, he said: “I’ve been quoted as saying that if I could have my way, I’d have a new newspaper for every street. That’s not impossible.”

Tindle, which is now run by Sir Ray’s son Owen, has struck a deal to sell its titles to a joint venture between Iliffe Media Group and the Fowler family.

The Iliffe family has been involved in publishing since 1891 and owns two of the oldest newspaper titles in the UK – the Rutland & Stamford Mercury and the Kentish Gazette – as well as local radio and TV stations.

Iliffe was also part-owner of The Telegraph in the 1920s and 1930s.

The Fowler family also has decades of experience in regional publishing. Peter Fowler, who died last year, was formerly the majority shareholder of Scottish Provincial Press, now known as Highland News & Media.

The two families currently run Highland News & Media as a joint venture. They said the deal for Tindle Newspapers would ensure the titles remained in private, family ownership with a long-term commitment to community-focused journalism.

Owen Tindle said: “This has not been an easy decision for my family.

“The business was built by my late father, and we are immensely proud of its rich heritage and the important role it has long played in the communities we serve.”

He added: “We believe this change of ownership represents the best strategy for our much-loved local brands to succeed in the long term.

“The titles will benefit from the greater combined resources of the new owners and their significant experience in developing sustainable digital subscription models.”

David Fordham, Iliffe’s non-executive director who spearheaded the acquisition, said: “Tindle’s newspapers have an exceptional heritage and a deep bond with their communities – values that closely align with our own.

“Our priority will be to accelerate digital subscription growth while preserving the trusted local reporting that readers rely on. We believe passionately that high-quality, independent local journalism is essential to strong, informed communities.”

Challenges for newspapers

The sale comes at a tumultuous time for local newspapers.

After grappling with the rise of tech giants that cannibalised digital advertising revenues, they are now rolling out AI tools that threaten to further dent audience numbers.

Around 300 local newspapers have closed over the last two decades, according to research by Press Gazette.

Reach, the newspaper group that owns titles such as the Manchester Evening News and Liverpool Echo, this month posted its biggest loss in a decade after suffering a sharp fall in referrals from Google.

The publisher, which has now launched subscriptions to reduce its reliance on advertising, has been accused of chasing clicks by running stories irrelevant to local readers.

Despite the challenges, some new local publishers have emerged in recent years to fill the gap.

These include Mill Media, which owns titles in Manchester, London, Liverpool and Birmingham, and London Centric, a newsletter founded by Jim Waterson, a former Guardian journalist.

data.title: NS&I kept £476m scandal secret for six months
data.description:
National Savings and Investments (NS&I) kept a £476m savings scandal secret for at least six months, The Telegraph can reveal.

NS&I officials became aware last June of a catastrophic operations failure under which money belonging to 37,500 dead savers was withheld from their families.

However, NS&I did not inform the Treasury until December. The Government waited a further three months to alert the public, only doing so after The Telegraph revealed the missing millions.

A second senior official at the bank stepped down on Friday amid growing fears of a cover-up. Ruth Curry, the bank’s people and finance director, is understood to have quit a day after Dax Harkins was forced out as chief executive.

The swift departure of the bank’s highest-ranking official – immediately followed by another member of the executive body – illustrates the scale of concern at the embattled bank and suggests a clean-up operation of its leadership is in full swing.

Critics have demanded officials clarify who knew what and when after it emerged that customers were complaining about NS&I long before the bank claimed to have become aware of the missing money.

The Telegraph has also found further evidence that the bank was aware of issues within the bereavement service for years.

NS&I presented a paper to the House of Commons in 2020 admitting failures relating to death claims in the bereavement service, saying there was “still room for improvement” and that the bank planned to make more changes.

Three years later, in June 2023, the bank investigated a surge in complaints linked to issues with families retrieving late relatives’ NS&I savings, ordering a report into the issue.

The board discussed an increase in complaints that had been upheld by the financial ombudsman, which was blamed in part on an increase in “death claims”.

Officials discussed how this was an area more likely to result in complaints and said that the issue would be covered in a forthcoming “conduct report”.

In October that year the board also referred to a “review” of bereavement claims issues.

A message to customers on the bank’s bereavement claims webpage now reads: “We’ve found that in some cases where a customer’s estate has made a bereavement claim, they didn’t receive the full amount owed to them.

“This happened because the search process we use to handle bereavement claims didn’t find all the savings accounts held by someone when they die.”

Ms Curry, whose team oversaw payments to customers as well as managing relationships with “key banking partners” and the Government, stood down on Friday as the fallout of the scandal continued.

She came from a government background with many years of experience including in the Cabinet Office where she championed transparency and accountability.

There she undertook a number of policy roles, including leading on Open Government – a governing doctrine where institutions share data and encourage public scrutiny.

She was also a clerk to the intelligence and security committee – which oversees MI5 and MI6 – and worked in the Office of Public Service Reform, a specialist unit within the Cabinet Office which focused on implementing improvements to public services such as health.

Accounts show she took home £235,000 last year, including £85,000 in pension perks, and a £15,000 bonus.

She is likely to have had contact with Lord Lemos, who was chairman of the NS&I board until he stepped down in July – the month that officials at the bank are understood to have realised the scale of the scandal.

NS&I insists his departure has nothing to do with the tracing failures, but the Conservatives and Reform are demanding answers.

Addressing MPs in the House Commons on Thursday, Torsten Bell, the pensions minister, said that “warning signs” had been missed repeatedly.

“What is now clear is that NS&I did not respond to those warning signs as fully as I, and more importantly, their customers, would expect. Nor did the last government act,” he said.

Mr Bell compared the crisis to “serious failings” by Santander in 2018 over processing the accounts and investments of deceased customers.

He added that after the Santander affair officials within NS&I “realised that this could pose problems for them”.

The bank is now also facing accusations of misleading MPs. In December, Mr Harkins appeared before the public accounts committee (PAC) to discuss NS&I’s beleaguered modernisation plan.

He told MPs the bank was delivering its customer service delivery metrics, but did not address the systemic tracing issue.

However, NS&I had privately informed the Treasury of the secret scandal that month. Sir Geoffrey Clifton-Brown, the chairman of the spending watchdog, told The Telegraph that he did not feel NS&I had been “forthcoming”.

“We did not know anything about this at that time. I do feel I was not fully informed [and] somebody might have been a bit more forthcoming. We simply knew nothing about it. Certainly had we known we would have asked him some questions about it,” he said.

Sarah Olney, a Liberal Democrat MP and PAC member, said Mr Harkins’s testimony to the committee did not give the “full picture” of problems at NS&I.

“He did not take the opportunity to be candid when he could have done,” she said. “I do think there was an opportunity to have addressed this issue that he did not take.”

Ms Olney said the questions MPs were asking Mr Harkins were “close enough” in subject matter to the savings scandal, which was then unknown to the public, to be relevant in his response.

Ms Olney added that she was “surprised” to have learned about it only now given that Mr Harkins had “been in front of us quite recently and we were challenging him on aspects of NS&I’s performance”.

NS&I did not comment on the departure of Ruth Curry, saying it did not comment on personal matters.

An NS&I spokesman said: “The issue was identified following a complaint relating to a bereavement claim in June last year. NS&I started work to determine the nature and scale of the issue and the NS&I Board, including HM Treasury, were provided with an initial brief in December 2025.

“Work has continued to address this complex issue, including through an extensive data validation exercise to assess the scope of the impact.”

Sir Jim Harra, who is acting as an emergency interim chief executive of the bank, will undertake a review over the next three months to spell out in detail the background to the scandal and “set out what lessons must be learned for NS&I going forward”.

The bank, which looks after £240bn for more than 24 million savers, has previously apologised for the failings.

data.title: British factory lines face shutdown within weeks
data.description:
British factories face production cuts within weeks because of surging gas prices, industry bodies have warned.

Ceramics businesses, glass manufacturers and chemical companies could bear the brunt of the global energy crisis because of a heavy reliance on gas, which has soared after attacks on Gulf energy facilities.

These sectors require large amounts of gas to power the high-temperature machines required to manufacture their goods – leaving them more vulnerable to the price spike.

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, said: “There’s a high risk that those factories exposed to the spot market prices will cut production in the next few weeks.

“We’ve seen a series of closures, particularly on the chemical side and ceramic side, and that was even before gas prices escalated a few weeks ago.”

Gas prices jumped by 11pc on Thursday after Iran carried out missile attacks on energy sites in Qatar and Saudi Arabia. European benchmark gas prices climbed to €60.72 per megawatt hour – their highest level since January 2023.

Mr Geveke warned that if energy prices remain at their recent highs the Government would need to introduce support within “weeks” or even sooner.

He added that ministers should prepare to intervene “in case this situation prolongs and prices increase further”.

Production cuts in British factories would have wider consequences for many supply chains, triggering knock-on effects across the economy.

The latest escalation in the Iran war adds to fears that companies already grappling with high taxes and levies could be put out of business by soaring energy costs.

Natural gas prices, a key input for UK electricity prices, have jumped by more than 90pc since the war in Iran began.

Frank Aaskov, from UK Steel, said some members had warned they would have to curtail production in the next few weeks.

‘Uncompetitive electricity prices’

He said that while the UK’s “uncompetitive industrial electricity prices have long been the primary barrier to the steel industry’s ability to compete internationally”, the situation was being exacerbated by escalating conflict in the Middle East.

The warnings add to the mounting pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, to unveil a package of support for companies grappling with a sharp increase in energy bills.

Nadine Bloxsome, the chief executive of the Aluminium Federation, said that unless the Government rolled out financial support measures there was a “real risk that UK manufacturing will become less competitive”.

She said: “UK aluminium manufacturers are already operating under significantly higher energy costs than many of our global competitors, and further increases driven by geopolitical instability only intensify that pressure.”

Any government intervention on energy price for businesses would be expensive. While gas prices are nowhere near the levels seen shortly after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, energy subsidies for businesses cost taxpayers an estimated £18.4bn in 2022.

Tom Jones, from the British Allied Trades Federation, said that the recent energy price spikes are “very destructive” for companies.

He added: “It seems that private industry, particularly manufacturing, takes the brunt of whatever comes along.”

The escalating attacks on energy facilities across the Gulf have led to fears that Britain will face oil and gas shortages in the coming months.

Mr Jones said members were concerned about the “impact of rationing that may come in if we can’t get enough gas or enough oil”.

A Government spokesman said: “We know this is a tough time for our energy intensive industries, who are grappling with increased fossil fuel prices. The best way to tackle this is getting onto home-grown power which we control to bring down bills for good.

“We have published a new steel strategy today that will boost advanced manufacturing across our economy, and we are working with all sectors to understand the impact of the situation in the Middle East and explore potential solutions.”

data.title: Supermarket petrol pumps run dry as drivers rush to fill up
data.description:
Supermarket petrol pumps have temporarily run out of fuel as motorists rush to fill up before prices surge higher.

The boss of Asda said the grocery giant had seen “significantly” higher demand for petrol, and as a result “the odd pump” at some stations had run dry.

Supermarkets are understood to have experienced higher than usual demand for fuel in recent weeks as nervous drivers fill their tanks in anticipation of further price rises.

The RAC said the price of unleaded petrol hit 150p per litre for the first time in almost two years on Friday, taking the cost of filling up a family car to about £80. The price of diesel is fast approaching 180p per litre.

Separate reports said industry insiders had warned ministers of a diesel shortage coming in mid-April. The Telegraph has not been able to verify the claim, which government insiders insisted they “did not recognise.”

Fuel industry sources also said they remained confident in supplies for at least another four weeks.

In a joint statement on Friday, Fuels Industry UK and the Petrol Retailers Association said: “We’re aware of reports circulating about fuel availability at a small number of forecourts for one retailer.

“Supply across the UK is flowing normally and there is no need for any change in usual buying habits.”

Motorists in India, South Africa, Australia and New Zealand have been panic-buying petrol in recent days, causing pump shortages even as national fuel supplies remain stable.

Russia ordered a ban on petrol exports on Friday following shortages that have been caused by Ukrainian attacks on Moscow’s oil refineries. The largest buyers of Russian gasoline are Turkey, China and Brazil.

Asda, which is the UK’s second-largest fuel retailer, admitted on Friday that it had run out of petrol at a small number of petrol pumps because of unusually high levels of demand in recent weeks.

Allan Leighton, Asda’s executive chairman, said: “Our fuel volumes are up quite significantly and clearly demand has been outstripping supply.

“Supply is tight and we are all trying hard on that. The issue is a temporary one, and some could see issues when we are waiting for delivery, and we can expect to see that continue.

“The spikiness at the moment makes this tricky for us, as spikes can lead to temporary shortages.

“These are temporary and are addressed very quickly.”

Petrol hits 150p per litre

Fuel Industry UK, a trade body, added: “Companies are monitoring the fuel supply situation closely and reporting regularly into the energy department on the status now and for the weeks ahead.

“Supply remains stable in the UK and our members are working hard to ensure that remains the case.”

Gordon Balmer, the executive director of the Petrol Retailers Association, said: “Industry and government remain in regular contact to monitor deliveries and stock levels in the UK as well as the situation internationally.”

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “The UK has a diverse and resilient energy supply.

“We continue to work with partners on the international situation.”

The price of petrol has now surged by 17.3p to 150.1p per litre since the US first attacked Iran at the end of February, according to the RAC. The price of diesel has also leapt by 35.3p to 177.7p per litre.

Average prices at motorway service stations are even higher, at 166p and 182p for unleaded and diesel respectively.

Simon Williams, the head of policy at the RAC, said: “With the long-awaited four-day Easter weekend almost within touching distance, the cost of getting away by car is going to be noticeably higher this year.

“Drivers on long journeys will need to plan very carefully where they refuel.

“The best advice remains to shop around for fuel and make use of free apps such as myRAC to never pay a penny more for fuel than is absolutely necessary.”

data.title: NS&I boss not required to have banking experience
data.description:
The former boss of National Savings and Investments (NS&I) was not required to have banking experience, adding to concerns over leadership at the embattled 160-year-old institution.

A 2023 job advertisement for the position of chief executive of NS&I, seen by The Telegraph, does not explicitly call for candidates to have ever worked in a bank.

Dax Harkins, the former NS&I boss, rose from NS&I’s sales team to become its chief executive that year. He had worked for an Australian mutual 20 years earlier.

He was forced out on Thursday after The Telegraph exposed a £467m savings scandal affecting up to 37,500 dead savers.

A Telegraph investigation revealed how savers had been denied money that was rightfully theirs, with the Government subsequently admitting to a systems failure that meant money had not been passed on to surviving relatives.

A clean-up operation of the leadership now appears to be in full swing, with the head of finance, Ruth Curry, also stepping down on Friday.

Despite providing services that are heavily regulated in the private sector, such as accepting deposits and managing investments, the state-owned bank is not legally required to follow the same rules that apply to commercial banks.

This includes strict governance requirements set by the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) that state the governing body of a firm must have an “appropriate range of skills and experience” to perform these activities.

When NS&I sought a new boss in 2023, it listed experience of the “retail financial services environment” as a third-place requirement after “strategic leadership” and managing a “transformation programme”.

Following the scandal exposed by the Telegraph this week, Sir Jim Harra, a career civil servant who formerly ran HMRC, has taken the reins.

Mr Harra joined the tax office in 1984 and is not known to have experience working in banking.

The former chairman of NS&I’s board, Lord Lemos, stepped down in the summer and has a background as a social policy researcher. 

He does not appear to have previously worked at a commercial bank but has held senior positions at banking bodies, including as Chair of the London Institute of Banking and Finance, Payments UK, the Lending Standards Board, the Banking Code Standards Board, and the Money Advice Service.

Mr Harra has also held positions on the boards of a number of arms-length bodies, including English Heritage, the Crown Prosecution Service and a string of financial trade associations.

‘NS&I allowed to act with impunity’

However, Neil Record, a former Bank of England economist, drew a distinction between the expertise of those who have worked for regulators and industry groups and those who have run commercial enterprises.

“There is a very deep built-in bias in the public sector to appoint people who they know and are knocking around the public service system,” he said.

“Most bankers have long careers and are not known in the public sector and have to take difficult decisions.

“I ran a financial services business for 40 years. Over the years, I have applied for three public service positions directly relevant to my experience and on none of those occasions was I given an interview.”

Mr Record claimed that NS&I not being required to follow FCA regulations allowed it to act with impunity.

He said: “Banks are enormously regulated and NS&I essentially isn’t because it’s the Government and they do what they want and therein lies the problem. They aren’t frightened of the regulator while commercial banks are terrified of it.”

An NS&I spokesman said: “NS&I’s Board combines deep financial services expertise alongside relevant experience across key areas such as retail, commercial and technology.”

data.title: Workers killed in fire ‘triggered by explosion’ at car factory
data.description:
Fourteen people have been killed and 59 injured in a fire at a car parts factory in South Korea, suspected to have been caused by an explosion.

Fire crews could not initially enter the factory, in the central city of Daejeon, because of the risk of the building collapsing and sodium stored at the site, which can explode if not properly handled.

More than 500 firefighters, police and emergency personnel were deployed to contain the fire and conduct rescue operations after it broke out at around 1pm local time on Friday.

Searches for the missing workers began late on Friday after officials deployed firefighting robots to cool the structure and conducted a safety inspection.

Around 120 vehicles and equipment including aircraft, an unmanned water cannon vehicle and two robots for hard-to-reach areas, were deployed.

The death toll was originally thought to be 11, but was updated after more bodies were found. Around 170 workers were at the plant when the fire broke out.

Officials have not said what may have caused the fire, which spread rapidly and was extinguished by Saturday afternoon. Witnesses said they had heard an explosion.

Videos and photos from the scene showed thick grey smoke billowing from the complex and some workers jumping from a building. Fire crews could be seen shooting water onto the site from cranes.

Some people were injured when they jumped from the building to escape, while others suffered from smoke inhalation, officials said. As of Saturday morning, 28 people were hospitalised and four of them underwent surgeries for broken bones and other injuries.

“We can confirm the latest death toll has risen to 14, with no one reported missing,” an official from the interior ministry’s department that handles fires and other disasters told AFP.

Police have identified one of the dead workers, while genetic testing is being conducted on others.

Lee Jae Myung, the South Korean president, visited the site and met bereaved families, promising to provide full support for victims and their families.

“The Government will thoroughly investigate the cause of the incident and prepare fundamental measures to prevent such tragedies,” she wrote on X.

Wealthy South Korea has a patchy record on labour safety, with more than 10,000 worksite deaths between 2000 and 2024, according to official statistics.

In September, South Korea sentenced the chief executive of Aricell, a battery maker, to 15 years in prison over one of the country’s worst industrial fires.

The 2024 blaze at the lithium battery factory in Hwaseong, south of the capital Seoul, killed 22 people, most of them Chinese nationals. In its verdict, the Suwon district court said the company had prioritised profit over workers’ safety.

data.title: Sir David Kirch, reclusive collector of rare banknotes who gave £100 million to the elderly of Jersey
data.description:
​Sir David Kirch, who has died 89, was a London property speculator who assembled what was thought to be Britain’s largest collection of provincial banknotes and the world’s most extensive personal collection of airship memorabilia; but he sold it all in the process of becoming one of the nation’s most generous philanthropists.

In the 1960s the reclusive Kirch had amassed a property empire that at one point included swathes of Kensington and Earl’s Court, as well as the Shaftesbury Theatre in the West End – which he threatened to demolish. Tall and elegant, he was viewed as an enigma. “Often at weekends, he takes his mother down to his cottage at Shoreham-on-Sea, otherwise he sits alone in his 15-room mansion in Hyde Park Gate and watches colour TV after eating his solitary dinner served by his Spanish butler,” reported the Evening Standard. In 1973 he settled as a tax exile in Jersey.

On reaching “three score and ten” in 2006, he decided to mark the milestone by distributing £100 Co-op vouchers at Christmas to every islander aged 70 and over – a commitment he honoured even on discovering that there would be 9,000 recipients. “I am fond of old people because I’m old myself,” he explained. He was delighted to learn that one pensioner had been able to buy a microwave oven, and called it “Mr Kirch”, so that “every time it pinged they thought of me”.

He repeated the scheme in subsequent years – later upping the voucher to £110 – and established a charitable trust to provide homes for pensioners on Jersey. In 2012, on being diagnosed with cancer, he announced that he would leave £100 million, the bulk of his fortune, to the elderly of Jersey.

Observing that his two happiest encounters with money were, first, in the making of it, and second, in the giving away, he proceeded to liquidate his assets to fund his trust. He sold his Rolls-Royce in favour of a Mini, and auctioned his collection of nearly 4,000 English provincial banknotes issued by defunct 19th-century institutions such as Scarborough Old Bank, Glastonbury & Shepton Mallet Bank and many others, their £5 and £10 denominations often fetching four-figure prices. He also sold a more unusual collection of “skit notes” – historic joke banknotes with political or satirical themes, issued by entities such as “the Bank of Love”.

His 15-ton collection of airship relics, sold in 2012, included medals awarded to the crew of the R101 which crashed in France in 1930 and a singed beer bottle salvaged from the wreckage of the Hindenburg in 1937.

David Roderick Kirch was born in Wimbledon on July 23 1936, the son of Leonard Kirch and his wife Margaret. His father had done well as a wholesale meat trader, but chose to keep travelling by train at six o’clock to Smithfield because, if the other traders saw his chauffeured Rolls-Royce, “he’d never get the price he wanted”.

At Tonbridge School, David demonstrated a flair for selling stamps. His commanding officer on National Service agreed that he would make a better businessman than a soldier, and since “I wasn’t clever enough to go to university,” as Kirch recalled, he found a job in the Lloyd’s insurance market.

On the untimely death of his father, aged 59, Kirch inherited £5,000 and launched himself in the meat trade. When his firm was taken over by a Dutch company, who wanted his Kingston cold store rather than his business, he realised that property was a better bet than meat, and bought a stake in a Hampshire estate agency.

He joined forces with his brother Peter in 1962, and they borrowed heavily to buy up chunks of west London, specialising in properties with sitting tenants that could be converted to more lucrative short-term lets. One flat in Courtfield Road was rented to the Rolling Stones; the police stripped off the oak panelling in the drawing room during a drugs bust.

Their first big gamble, as Kirch told an interviewer, was to buy 23 houses in Earl’s Court for more than £250,000 – 90 per cent of it borrowed – though they were producing rental income of only £1,000 a year. “Once you had dealt with sums of a quarter of a million,” he observed, “things do become much easier.”

In the early 1970s the Kirch brothers bought into a quoted company, Peureula (Sumatra) Rubber Estates, which they renamed Peureula Investments and through which they amassed unpayable debts of some £35 million – chiefly to Keyser Ullman, one of the fringe banks heaviest hit by the property crash of 1973. “They can only ever hope to get their money back,” Kirch said in 1976, “which they won’t.”

Having lost nearly everything, David Kirch later rebuilt his portfolio to a value at one point estimated at £250 million.

His enthusiasms as a collector ranged from glass eyebaths to memorabilia from the Crystal Palace (which he dreamed of rebuilding) and a bench that once belonged to Lord Lucan. One particular indulgence was cars; another was gambling. At the Casino de la Fôret at Le Touquet, Ian Fleming challenged him to a race back to their hotel in their sports cars.

Being a covert homosexual, Kirch recalled, was another form of gambling: “Sex was that much more exciting when it was illegal.” He regretted never having children, and observed that his life had spanned an era when his friend, Lord Montagu, could be imprisoned for homosexuality, to the legalisation of gay marriage and adoption by same-sex couples.

In 2013 David Kirch was appointed KBE.

His long-term partner Serge Cuhat died in 2009 in an Alpine ballooning accident.

Sir David Kirch, born July 23 1936, died February 28 2026​

data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: ‘Rogue builders ran off with £200k and left me homeless’
data.description:
Have you lost money to rogue builders? Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Steve Higgs never envisioned that six years after buying his forever home in Surrey, he would find himself living in a plasterboard shack and £200,000 out of pocket.

But when the building company he hired to redevelop his £625,000 property claimed it had gone bust and abruptly ceased all communication with him, he was left with nothing but foundations and a few half-finished walls despite handing over £170,000 for the work.

Mr Higgs, 39, had set out to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom property in Horley, after he was advised that it had serious asbestos issues.

Since early 2022, he has been forced to live in a makeshift cabin on the grounds of his three-acre estate as his property became uninhabitable.

He last had contact with the building company responsible in January 2025 when they told him they were experiencing “financial troubles” after his home had been demolished.

Mr Higgs, who was paralysed from the chest down in a motorcycle accident when he was 26, said the ordeal had left him emotionally drained, homeless and facing a big hole in his finances.

He said: “It has played havoc on my mental health. They’ve already wasted four years of my life and £170,000. I can’t sit on my hands in this little box any more. I’ve got no windows. It’s just plasterboard walls. I’m literally just living in a cell of my own making.”

It comes as Britain faces a plague of rogue builders that is costing homeowners and the economy billions of pounds a year.

In the past five years alone, homeowners lost an estimated £14.3bn to cowboy builders, while a fear of being ripped off has cost the economy a further £10bn in lost jobs, according to the Federation of Master Builders (FMB) trade body.

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, said the cost of living crisis had created the “perfect environment” for sham building companies to proliferate.

He said: “There are no barriers to entry so it’s easy to come in and scam people. The rogue builder will come along, undercut a decent builder and then, because people are short of cash, they will go for the cheap option. And it’s only getting worse.”

Mr Higgs paid the building company £113,000 to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom house in Horley, Surrey, after he was advised that issues with asbestos and rotten timber frames made it more cost-effective than his original plan to renovate and extend.

He paid an additional £55,000 for roof truss installation and spent £30,000 on buying materials directly from a roofing supplier.

He said the building company had a strong online presence, including a professional website, Facebook reviews and a range of photos displaying previous work, as well as a branded fleet of vans. Up to four labourers a day would appear on site.

However, when Mr Higgs raised concerns about the quality of bricklaying and incorrect proportions for the new foundations, he was told, in correspondence seen by The Telegraph, that the company had “run into financial troubles”.

Despite numerous calls and emails the company stopped responding to queries about when the remedial work would be carried out.

After a few months, he hired a lawyer to draft a letter before action for £7,000, only to be advised that it could cost up to £120,000 pursuing the claim in a civil court.

He was told that even if he won there was no guarantee he would be able to recoup what he paid out as the company was claiming financial distress.

‘Hopeless’ legal process

Currently all builders carrying out work on private homes are regulated by the Consumer Rights Act 2015. It sets out the standards that consumers can expect when a trader supplies building works, and statutory remedies if those rights are breached.

However, if the company is declared insolvent, options for reclaiming money can be extremely limited and outcomes are typically poor.

Mark Garnier, shadow economic secretary, who has campaigned for more stringent controls on building firms, has previously called the legal process for bringing rogue traders to justice “hopeless”.

If the work was paid for using a credit card, homeowners can claim back up to £30,000 under Section 75. But many rogue traders encourage cash-in-hand payments at a discount to avoid creating a paper trail.

Paula Higgins, at the Homeowners’ Alliance trade body, said “phoenix companies” – whereby the owner of an insolvent business simply rebrands under a different name – was an ongoing problem in the construction sector.

She said: “If you investigate certain directors, they will be setting companies up and closing them down all the time – [the regulation] around it is very weak.”

Despite calls from industry leaders for tighter regulation such as a mandatory national licensing scheme ensuring minimum competency for all builders, the Government has dragged its heels on introducing any new legislation.

Mr Gray said the Government didn’t appear to have any plans to implement controls in the construction sector despite “zero protections” for consumers against rogue builders.

He said: “Anyone can call themselves a builder because they’re not regulated. In certain circumstances you can go through the court, but ultimately if the company folds there’s nothing you can do. You can’t claim anything back. You just have to go to the drawing board.”

To avoid falling prey to rogue builders, Ms Higgins said the most important steps were to get a contract in place, avoid paying for projects upfront and in cash, and to carry out extensive research into the company beyond its own website and social media channels.

She also warned against any company that is available at short notice. “The best builders are the ones you have to wait for,” she said, adding that everyone should look on Companies House to see for how long a business has been operating.

For word of mouth recommendations, ensure their previous work is similar to what you are asking for and check previous projects yourself, she added.

Mr Higgs said he had been quoted £23,000 for remedial work, including widening the existing foundation to the correct size. The rest of the project is expected to cost another £90,000, plus VAT.

“It’s pretty much looking at the same figures again,” he said.

Mr Higgs receives an income from a personal injury trust, but said the financial impact would be long-lasting. “It’s definitely thrown a spanner in the works,” he said. “It’s turned what was meant to be a five-year build plan into a 10-year plan.”

A spokesman for the Department for Business and Trade said: “Rogue builders are a scourge for hard-working families. Anyone who is affected can contact their local authority who have new powers to hand out tougher fines against businesses who rip off consumers.”

data.title: £50bn construction giant abandons London stock market
data.description:
Building materials giant CRH is to quit the London Stock Exchange (LSE) in a further blow to Britain’s standing as a global financial hub.

CRH, which is worth nearly £54bn, said it had decided to exit the UK market because it was hamstrung by high regulatory costs and low levels of trading activity.

The firm’s shares will now be traded only in New York, where they had been listed in addition to London since September 2023.

The move serves as yet another setback for London’s struggling stock market, with CRH joining a string of high-profile companies that have defected to New York in search of higher valuations.

Online payments company Wise, worth £12bn, last year transferred its main share listing to the US, saying the move would allow better access to the world’s most liquid stock market.

Meanwhile, AstraZeneca said in September that it would upgrade its presence on the New York exchange in what bosses described as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

While the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain based in the UK, the announcement has stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker and most valuable business could ultimately use the move as a stepping stone to quitting the LSE.

CRH had already declared New York as its primary listing and had been removed from the FTSE 100 index.

The company, which is based in Dublin, said a review launched last month had concluded that quitting London completely was “in the best interests of CRH and its shareholders”.

It said: “CRH carefully considered, among other factors, the level of trading activity for its ordinary shares on the LSE as well as the additional cost, regulatory and administrative obligations arising from retaining the LSE listings.”

The firm said it had asked the Financial Conduct Authority to cancel its London listing, with the move expected to be completed by Apr 20.

CRH said on switching to the dual listing three years ago that the decision had also been partly driven by a US push for onshoring manufacturing activity, something that has gathered pace under Donald Trump.

CRH, which was founded in 1970 via a merger of two Irish cement and quarrying companies, already counts North America as its biggest market.

data.title: Savannah Guthrie: I wake up imagining my missing mother’s terror
data.description:
Savannah Guthrie has said she wakes up every night imagining the “terror” her mother must have gone through during her suspected kidnapping.

The US morning news anchor made the comments in her first interview since her mother, Nancy Guthrie, went missing at the end of January.

The presenter struggled to hold back tears as she told her NBC Today co-host Hoda Kotb: “To think of what she went through. I wake up every night in the middle of the night.

“And in the darkness, I imagine her terror. And it is unthinkable, but those thoughts demand to be thought.”

She added that her family was “in agony” over the disappearance of the 84-year-old.

Mrs Guthrie was last seen at 9.45 pm on Jan 31, when her family dropped her off at her home just outside of Tucson, Arizona. She was reported missing the following day.

The case has puzzled investigators, who have turned up few clues about her whereabouts.

Ten days into the search, authorities released a video of a masked, armed figure who appeared to tamper with a security camera at Nancy’s home in the early hours of Feb 1. But law enforcement has not yet been able to identify them.

Drops of Mrs Guthrie’s blood were found on her front porch. Her phone, Apple Watch and vital medications were also still inside the home.

Chris Nanos, the Pima County sheriff who is overseeing the investigation, said DNA from an unknown source had also been found.

Ransom notes sent to media outlets in the days after her disappearance appeared to chart a possible path to Nancy’s safe return.

Savannah Guthrie posted a video of herself and her siblings to her Instagram account and addressed the possible kidnappers directly. They were “ready to talk,” she said. But their calls appear to have gone unanswered.

The Guthrie family has since offered a $1m (£747,000) reward for Nancy’s recovery.

“Someone needs to do the right thing. We are in agony. We are in agony. It is unbearable,” Savannah said in a clip of the interview with Ms Kotb broadcast on Wednesday.

NBC will air more of the interview on Thursday and Friday.

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Seven common home improvements that won’t pay off when you sell
data.description:
If you’re considering selling your home this year, you might be tempted to fix everything you can before putting it on the market.

Whether it’s replacing your outdated kitchen or getting new carpets, many sellers think a bit of investment will help secure a higher price or a quicker sale. But some fixes simply won’t pay off.

Certain improvements will waste both your time and money, and may well get replaced by the new owner as soon as they move in.

Angela Kerr, from the HomeOwners Alliance, said: “Home buyers realise a home has been loved and lived in, so will have some wear and tear. While in an ideal world, your property would look perfect, you need to prioritise spending money on essential repairs and minor redecoration that will help your home sell faster.”

To maximise value and minimise stress, knowing where to hold back on improvements may be just as useful as knowing where to take action.

Here, Telegraph Money has asked the experts which common property fixes sellers shouldn’t bother spending money on.

1. A kitchen replacement

Think twice before going all out and installing a shiny new kitchen, especially if your current one is functional.

There’s a risk you could spend thousands on this outlay shortly before selling, only to discover it does little to increase your home’s value.

Tom Evans, sales director at estate agency Purplebricks, said: “Most buyers prefer to personalise this area for themselves. As long as it works – and isn’t too shabby or flimsy – I would advise against the time and expense of upgrading.”

If you’re looking for a quick fix in the kitchen that won’t break the budget, consider replacing just the cabinet doors, handles or worktops to give your existing room a bit of a revamp.

2. Adding an extension

Another common mistake sellers make before putting their property on the market is going all out on a loft conversion or extension.

Marc von Grundherr, director of estate agency Benham and Reeves, said: “What sellers should avoid is embarking on expensive projects purely to try to increase the sale price in the short term. Major extensions, loft conversions or structural layout changes rarely make financial sense if the only goal is to sell the property immediately afterwards.”

While projects like this can add value, they can also be very pricey – not to mention time-consuming.

Mr Evans said: “An expensive conversion is unlikely to ever correspond with a particularly elevated property price. There is a risk that if you, say, spend £50,000 on the extension, it might only add £40,000 to the property.”

Another big no-no is “over-improving” the area.

Mr Evans added: “If your home is already at the top price range for your street, there is little point in putting more time and money into the property pre-sale.”

3. Redesigning the garden

If you’re considering giving your outside area an overhaul, think again.

Mr Evans said: “Another area people tend to really push the boat out on is garden design. There’s a dizzying array of things you could do to your garden, and an eye-watering sum you could plough into making it look great. But from experience, this rarely makes a difference to the asking price – or the speed or amount the property sells for.”

Of course, you shouldn’t neglect your garden, either. But rather than spending money on a redesign that isn’t worth the time or money, carrying out smaller, more manageable tasks, such as tidying lawns, weeding flower beds and repairing fences.

It’s all about “kerb appeal”, according to Dominick Brown, director of buying agency Prime Purchase.

He said: “Think about that all-important first impression or kerb appeal. Is the driveway clear? Does it need additional gravel to fill in bumps? Does the house present as well as it possibly can at first glance? See if there are any slipped tiles which need to be corrected, bins hidden, and flower beds planted or tidied.”

4. New white goods

While you might like the idea of potential buyers setting eyes on a top-of-the-range fridge, freezer or dishwasher when they come to view your home, buying these appliances could be a waste of money.

Ms Kerr said: “You’re unlikely to recoup what you’ve spent when you sell. If you need to replace an appliance because it’s broken, consider a second-hand [one] to keep costs down.”

5. A bathroom upgrade

As with your kitchen, there’s a risk that if you pour lots of cash into bathroom renovations, it could be money down the drain.

Mr Evans said: “There’s no point replacing a perfectly usable bathroom suite with an expensive one. If you do, there’s every chance the potential buyer will have different views on what their dream bathroom might be.”

As long as the taps and fixtures are working properly – and you don’t have any leaks – you are better off focusing your efforts on tasks such as deep cleaning, regrouting tiles and replacing worn flooring.

6. Expensive home improvements

While it’s not a bad idea to give your home a fresh lick of paint if needed, be sparing, as doing too much could end up putting buyers off.

Your choice of decor is important, too. Give bold colours a wide berth, and focus instead on using just a fresh coat of neutral paint to make your home feel lighter and brighter.

Mr Evans said: “Any home improvement that involves personal taste – or which is a trend – is not worth adjusting. If the imperfections are just cosmetic, and something that the buyer can live with, we would recommend leaving well alone.”

7. New carpets

If your existing carpets feel old and worn, you may be dying to refresh your home with brand new ones. But once again, this is not a sensible use of your cash.

Ms Kerr said: “Instead, for a much cheaper fix, get your carpets deep cleaned. You’ll be amazed at the difference it makes.”

You can either pay a professional to do the job for you. Or, to keep the costs down further still, you could hire the equipment and do the job yourself.

What is worth spending money on?

When preparing a home for sale, it’s important to spend time and effort on the things that genuinely reassure a buyer, rather than shelling out on upgrades that they may not like or will not be reflected in the sale price.

Mr von Grundherr said: “The priority should always be the fundamentals of the property. Issues such as damp, visible structural problems, electrical faults, heating issues or missing roof tiles are all immediate red flags because they raise concerns about the wider condition of the home.

“Even relatively minor problems, such as cracked plaster, can make house-hunters question whether there are deeper issues beneath the surface.”

Addressing these types of repairs before listing can make a big difference to buyer confidence.

Amy Reynolds, from estate agency Antony Roberts, said: “The key is to focus on issues that could derail a sale – rather than chasing perfection. If the rest of the property is in good condition, it is usually worth honing in on issues that regularly trigger down-valuations, renegotiations or even collapsed sales.”

This includes any problems that may get flagged up in a survey.

Mr Brown said: “For example, any lingering damp is likely to raise concerns for both buyers and their surveyors. Failing to remedy it will almost certainly become an issue during price negotiations or following a survey.”

Roof repairs are another no-brainer; you need to get these issues fixed as they will be visible to a buyer before they even step in the door, whether they get a building survey or not.

Remember the small ‘fixes’

If you are trying to move quickly, there are plenty of smaller tasks you can do yourself, which can improve the appeal of your home – without significant expense. Decluttering and deep cleaning are a great starting point.

Ms Reynolds said: “Focus on presentation – fresh paint, good lighting and declutter.”

Get rid of limescale, clean and repair tile grout, and wax wooden floors.

It’s not all about what a property looks like; make sure it smells good too. Ms Kerr said: “Try different products to get your bathroom to shine, get rid of odours, hang up fresh towels. This will make the place more appealing and allow potential buyers to imagine living there.”

Mr Evans added: “Whether it’s touching up scuffed paintwork, filling in small cracks in the plastering, replacing broken handles or light fittings, re-sealing bathrooms and kitchens, or deep cleaning carpets – a neutral, clean and well-maintained property will sell better than a stylish but tired one.”

If you’re not sure which jobs to prioritise before putting your home on the market, speak to an estate agent about which fixes are likely to make your home more likely to sell.

Ms Kerr said: “If you want to know if anything in your home is a potential deal breaker, then ask a good local estate agent. They will know what prospective buyers in your area are looking for.”

data.title: Best cash Isas: Today’s latest rates
data.description:
A cash Isa is similar to a savings account, but with a crucial difference – you don’t pay tax on the interest you earn.

It is one of four types of adult individual savings accounts (Isas) – the others are stocks and shares Isas, innovative finance Isas and lifetime Isas.

You can currently save up to £20,000 per tax year, which is known as the Isa allowance. In the 2025 Budget, Chancellor Rachel Reeves announced plans to reduce this to £12,000 from April 2027 for savers under the age of 65; younger savers will have to use an investment Isa if they wish to use the remaining £8,000 of their allowance.

You can either deposit this sum into one account or spread it across several, and you can transfer your Isa savings between providers if you want to take advantage of a better rate elsewhere.

In this guide, Telegraph Money explains how cash Isas work, covering:

  • How we determine the best rates
  • The best cash Isas in March
  • Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa
  • Why choose a cash Isa?
  • Cash Isa FAQs

How we determine the best rates

The Best Buy tables below show the best savings rates widely available in the market. This means certain accounts are excluded, including those that are available only to local or existing customers.

The data in these tables is provided by Savings Data Limited and is compiled using automated tracker tools and updates from savings providers. Savings Data Limited then manually checks the information and enters it into a database that feeds the live tables, which update daily.

The savings accounts shown are protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). The information in this article is intended for information purposes and should not be taken as endorsement or advice.

The best cash Isas in March 2026

Find the best cash Isa for you using the tables below.

Best easy-access cash Isas

These accounts usually allow you to make as many deposits and withdrawals as you like – but this flexibility can come with lower rates. For the savings equivalent, you can see our guide to the best easy-access savings accounts.

Best variable rate cash Isas

Accounts in the table below show the best Isa rates on the market for accounts that offer a variable rate of interest. While this could increase at any time – which can be valuable when rates are on an upward trend – it could also decrease.

Best notice cash Isas

These accounts require you to give a certain amount of notice to your provider when you want to make a withdrawal, indicating the number of days you must wait until your money is released. You can usually make as many withdrawals as you like – but be sure to check the account terms first.

If this way of saving suits you, make sure you check our guide to the best notice accounts for alternative options.

Best fixed-rate cash Isas

These Isas require you to lock your money away for a specified period of time. The rate of interest is guaranteed throughout this period, which can give you peace of mind should other rates take a tumble.

For the savings account equivalent, which you might want to use if you’ve used up your Isa allowance, see our guide to the best fixed-rate bonds.

For other types of tax-free saving, see our guide to the best Junior cash Isas for under-18s. Or, if you’re saving to buy a home or fund your retirement, don’t miss our guide to the best lifetime Isas.

Expert opinion: Things to consider when choosing a cash Isa

Choosing the best cash Isa for you can be tricky, as it depends on why you’re saving and how you want to save – but there should be options to suit everyone.

Caitlyn Eastell, of analyst Moneyfactscompare, said: “For savers that are more tax savvy, cash Isas may be the ideal option as you receive a £20,000 Isa allowance each year and can pay into multiple Isas within the same period providing this limit is not exceeded. Isas aren’t too dissimilar to their traditional savings counterparts with easy access, notice, fixed and regular accounts being an option.

“It is worth considering that for the variable rate options, interest could increase or decrease over time, and those Isas that are fixed for an agreed term will either not allow earlier access or may deduct a certain amount of interest.”

Cash Isas can also be an effective way of saving for the longer term.

Mark Hicks, of Hargreaves Lansdown, said: “Cash Isas have certainly seen a resurgence of late. Looking at what may be coming in Labour’s Budget, and the rhetoric around higher taxes, cash Isas should be the first port of call for all savers at the moment.

“If you’ve got £20,000 or less, definitely go for a cash Isa first and ensure your money is protected from tax. If you’re fortunate enough to have more than £20,000 to save in an Isa, then you can look at other saving accounts as well.”

Why choose a cash Isa?

Cash Isas are beneficial for many savers as they offer an easy way to make sure your savings interest is sheltered from income tax.

Their popularity took a dip when the personal savings allowance (PSA) was introduced in 2016, as it means some savers can earn up to £1,000 in tax-free savings interest each tax year. When interest rates are low, only those with large savings pots or high incomes come into the scope of paying tax on savings interest.

However, savings rates have been high for some time; until recently it was not uncommon to be earning 5pc. Someone with a £1,000 PSA could face a savings tax bill with £20,000 in savings – a problem that doesn’t occur with a cash Isa. What’s more, your growth is protected as it compounds for years into the future, which has helped some savers become Isa millionaires.

The main issue that comes with a cash Isa is the £20,000 Isa limit on how much you can pay into it each tax year. If you want to move a large savings deposit in order to protect your returns from tax, it may need to be moved gradually over several tax years.

In some cases, interest rates offered by cash Isas can be lower than their savings equivalent. Providers are well aware of the extra tax benefit Isas offer, and know they don’t have to be as competitive with rates.

It’s important to shop around and find the most competitive rate you can. Cash Isa rates can be lower than their savings account counterparts, but the tax-free benefits of an Isa will often outweigh the loss of a little interest.

If you’re not sure whether you could be in danger of having to pay tax on your savings interest, try the Telegraph Money savings tax calculator – simply enter your savings, interest rate and tax band and it can tell you whether you’d be better off getting an Isa.

Cash Isa FAQs

There is no limit to the number of cash Isas you can have – but it’s a good idea not to have so many that you lose track of them.

You can hold cash Isas you’ve paid into in past tax years, and – now that Isa rules have changed – you can also open and pay into as many as you like in the same tax year. The key thing is to make sure you don’t pay in more than £20,000 across all Isas in the same tax year.

Yes, you can combine Isas into one account – but in order to do this without affecting the tax status of the money, or your Isa allowance, you must move the cash via an Isa transfer.

There are quite a few rules to bear in mind when you do this – you can find out more in our comprehensive guide to Isa transfers.

This depends on the rate you’re earning and the rate of inflation. At the time of writing, lots of cash Isas can beat inflation, but you might need to shop around for them. It’s a good idea to keep an eye on what’s happening with inflation versus the best Isa rates so you can gauge whether or not you have a competitive deal.

Our inflation calculator shows the effect rising prices can have on your savings.

It’s never too late to put money in an Isa to benefit from its tax-free status. However, if you’re planning to deposit money very close to the end of the tax year on April 5, there’s a chance you could miss the deadline.

If this happens, you can still save the money – it will just be taken from the following year’s allowance instead.

The annual Isa allowance resets at the start of a tax year on April 6. You can choose to put this entire amount into one account, or split it among several.

Mr Hicks said: “Isas were typically seasonal, with a big spike in demand around the end of the tax year. They are much less timely now and every year the cash Isa season gets longer and longer.”
